items.title: Author Correction: How mood-related physiological states bias economic decisions
items.author: Mathias Pessiglione
items.content: Author Correction: How mood-related physiological states bias economic decisions

items.title: Subjective valuation as a domain-general process in creative thinking
items.author: Alizée Lopez-Persem
items.content: Using computational modeling of behavioral data, this study demonstrate that preferences involved in creative thinking are stable across three domains of creativity.

items.title: Pledges counteract the boomerang effect in a sustainable showering intervention for children
items.author: Zhiyu Wang
items.content: A randomized control trial in Singapore finds that private and public pledges reduced kids’ shower times, countering a boomerang effect from a 5 min goal. Both pledges boosted motivation, saving water and promoting lasting conservation habits.

items.title: Liberals and conservatives respond divergently to stereotype portrayals of race and gender
items.author: Margaret J. Shih
items.content: Across four experiments, findings indicate that conservatives and liberals divergently evaluate stereotype portrayals of race and gender, displaying both consistency and variability in their preference for stereotypes depicted by racial minority or white male and female models.

items.title: Social aloofness is associated with non-social explore-exploit decisions
items.author: Alexander B. Herman
items.content: High social aloofness was linked to reduced exploration, lower decision noise, and high choice stickiness in a bandit task. These effects reflect habitual and outcome-driven behaviours, linking social disengagement to nonsocial decision flexibility.

items.title: Cognitive distortions are associated with increasing political polarization
items.author: Matteo Serafino
items.content: This study shows that rising political polarization is linked to an increase in distorted language patterns—commonly associated with anxiety and depression—highlighting a link between political expression and cognitively distorted thinking

items.title: Range nudges enhance behavioural adherence to safety and health guidelines
items.author: Kazuhiro Ueda
items.content: Explicitly presenting a range (e.g. 0–60 km/h) instead of a single limit (e.g. 60 km/h)—the ‘Range Nudge’—acts as an additional anchor to enhance adherence. Seven experiments showed reduced speeding and increased handwashing time.

items.title: Fake news, real war
items.author: Jais Adam-Troian
items.content: Misinformation is often framed as a cognitive failure, focusing on the vulnerabilities of those who believe it. But misinformation often stems from deliberate disinformation campaigns—which should be considered proactive intergroup aggression.
